# Prompting, Informed by the Literature {#sec-prompting}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
**🚧 Status: `drafted`** — a machine-assisted draft built from my teaching materials, awaiting my full revision. Read it as a working paper, not a finished chapter — and tell me what breaks in the comments below.
:::

In 1941, [Donald Rugg ran a split-ballot experiment](https://academic.oup.com/poq/article-abstract/5/1/91/1866582) in *Public Opinion Quarterly*. Half the respondents were asked whether the United States should *allow* public speeches against democracy; the other half, whether it should *forbid* them. Same policy, logically equivalent questions. When asked about allowing, 75 percent said no. When asked about forbidding, only 54 percent said yes. Twenty-one points of daylight between two wordings of one question.

You already know this problem. Survey researchers have spent more than eighty years learning that the instrument shapes the measurement. A prompt is an instrument. When you use an LLM to code parliamentary speeches, survey open-ends, or interview transcripts, the prompt *is* your questionnaire, your codebook, and your coder training session, all in one block of text — and it has wording effects at least as large as Rugg's. This chapter is about writing that instrument well, and it is deliberately literature-driven: prompting is drowning in folklore, and you should be able to tell which techniques have evidence behind them, which are superstition, and how to treat your prompt with the same care you would treat a survey question. The previous chapter (@sec-apis) built the plumbing; here we decide what to send through it. We keep the same running example: coding parliamentary speeches on immigration as *restrictive*, *expansive*, or *neutral*.

## What a prompt actually does

Strip away the mysticism first. Recall from @sec-transformers that a language model does one thing: given a sequence of tokens, it produces a probability distribution over the next token. A prompt is not a command the model obeys. It is context that conditions that distribution. When you write "You are a political scientist coding speeches," you are not hiring a political scientist; you are shifting probability mass toward the kinds of continuations that followed such text in training data.

This deflationary view is useful because it predicts both why prompting works and why it is fragile. It works because instructions, examples, and formats genuinely shift the distribution toward the outputs you want. It is fragile because *everything* in the context shifts the distribution — including things you did not intend as signal: the order of your examples, whether you used bullet points or commas, whether you said "Hello" first. Keep both halves in mind. Everything in this chapter follows from them.

## What reliably works

The prompting literature is enormous — [Schulhoff et al. (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.06608) catalogue 58 distinct techniques from 1,565 papers — but for research annotation tasks, four moves carry most of the weight, and each has actual evidence behind it.

**Clear task definition.** The single highest-return move is boring: say precisely what the task is, what the categories are, what the unit of analysis is, and what the output should look like. This is not a machine-learning insight; it is coder training. It is also how the benchmark work proceeds: [Ziems et al. (2024)](https://aclanthology.org/2024.cl-1.8/), evaluating LLMs across 25 computational social science tasks, build each task's prompt from the dataset's original annotation instructions, constrain the model to the codebook's label set, and average results over paraphrased prompts because instruction wording alone moves the numbers. A model told "code this speech's stance on immigration" is guessing at your construct. A model given your category definitions is at least guessing at the right one.

**Few-shot examples.** Include a handful of worked examples — input, then correct label — before the document to be coded. This is the founding result of the prompting literature: [Brown et al. (2020)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165) showed that GPT-3 could pick up new tasks from examples in the prompt alone, no retraining, which is why the technique is also called *in-context learning*. For annotation, examples do two jobs definitions cannot: they pin down borderline cases ("this hedged speech counts as restrictive") and they demonstrate the output format by existence proof. The caveat is serious, though: few-shot performance is unstable. [Zhao et al. (2021)](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/zhao21c/zhao21c.pdf) showed that the *order* of examples and the class balance among them can swing accuracy dramatically — models drift toward the label of the most recent example and toward labels that are common in the examples. Balance your classes, and check robustness to example order (more on that below).

**Output schemas.** Tell the model exactly what shape the answer takes — ideally enforce it with the structured-output tools from @sec-apis. A constrained output space ("exactly one of: restrictive, expansive, neutral") does more than save parsing code. It stops the model from inventing category 4 on document 7,342, and it turns a generation task into something much closer to classification.

**Decomposition.** When the coding decision is genuinely complex — "is this speech populist?" — break it into smaller questions and ask them separately or in sequence: Does the speech invoke 'the people' as a homogeneous body? Does it frame elites as corrupt? Then combine with an explicit rule. Small, well-defined judgments are where models are most reliable, and decomposition also gives you intermediate outputs you can audit. The related, more famous technique is **chain-of-thought**: asking the model to reason step by step before answering, either with worked reasoning examples ([Wei et al., 2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903)) or with the bare instruction "Let's think step by step" ([Kojima et al., 2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.11916)). Both papers show large gains — *on arithmetic and symbolic reasoning benchmarks*. Read the fine print before you copy the technique: a meta-analysis of over 100 papers ([Sprague et al., 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.12183)) finds that chain-of-thought helps mainly on math and logic, with small-to-no gains on most other task types — and classification is mostly "other." For a stance-coding task, chain-of-thought buys you longer, costlier outputs and, usually, similar labels. Use it when the task genuinely requires multi-step inference; skip it when it does not.

::: {.callout-warning}
If you do elicit reasoning, do not treat the model's stated rationale as an explanation of its decision. The rationale is more text sampled from the same distribution — plausible-sounding justification, not a window into the computation. It can be useful for auditing (a rationale citing text that is not in the speech is a red flag), but "the model explained its label" is a reading, not a proof. What it would even mean to see the real computation is the subject of the interpretability chapters, starting with @sec-why-interpretability.
:::

## What is folklore

Now the other pile. A large share of circulating prompt advice — assign the model an impressive persona, offer it a tip, tell it to take a deep breath, threaten it, say please — has a weak or contradictory evidence base. [Salinas and Morstatter (2024)](https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-acl.275/) tested perturbations like these across eleven classification tasks and found that they *do* change outputs — even opening with "Hello!" shifted predictions — but not in reliably helpful directions. That is the worst of both worlds: real sensitivity, no dependable gain. The [Prompt Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.06608) reaches a similar verdict across the wider literature: many named techniques have never been tested outside the narrow benchmark they were introduced on, and results frequently fail to transfer across models and tasks.

Two clarifications so this stays fair. First, role instructions are not useless — "you are a political scientist applying the codebook below" plausibly helps set register and vocabulary, and it costs nothing. What lacks evidence is the folklore version, where "20 years of experience" or "world-renowned expert" is supposed to buy accuracy. Second, folklore is not harmless. Every untested incantation you paste into a prompt is a researcher degree of freedom you cannot justify to a reviewer, and — as the sensitivity results below show — it may be quietly moving your measurements. My rule: everything in a research prompt should have a reason you could state out loud. If the reason is "a LinkedIn post said it helps," cut it.

<!-- DRAFT NOTE (Emre): classroom story goes here — the semester students compared their "expert persona" prompts against plain ones and what actually happened. -->

## By hand: a weak prompt and a strong one

Take one speech — invented, but the kind of hedged text that makes human coding teams argue:

> "No one doubts that immigration has enriched this country, and nobody on these benches wants to slam the door. But compassion without control is not a policy, and the public knows it."

The weak prompt is the one everyone writes first:

```
What do you think about this speech? [speech]
```

Send that and you get an essay: three paragraphs on the rhetorical strategy of concession, a note about "balancing perspectives," no label, different every time. Not wrong, exactly — just useless. You cannot put an essay in a dataframe, and you asked for an opinion, so you got one.

The strong prompt does five jobs, and it is worth seeing them labeled:

```
[ROLE]        You are a political scientist coding parliamentary
              speeches using the codebook below.

[TASK]        Code the speech's stance toward immigration levels.

[CODEBOOK]    restrictive — favors reducing immigration or tightening
              controls, including hedged or "compassionate" framings
              whose policy direction is restrictive.
              expansive — favors maintaining or increasing immigration,
              or expanding rights of immigrants.
              neutral — purely procedural, descriptive, or genuinely
              balanced with no discernible policy direction.
              Decision rule: code the policy direction, not the tone.
              If no policy direction is discernible, code neutral.

[FORMAT]      Return JSON: {"stance": ..., "evidence": one short quote
              from the speech}

[CONSTRAINTS] stance must be exactly one of: restrictive, expansive,
              neutral. The evidence quote must appear verbatim in
              the speech.
```

Now the model returns `{"stance": "restrictive", "evidence": "compassion without control is not a policy"}` — because the codebook's decision rule ("policy direction, not tone") resolves exactly the ambiguity this speech was built on. The concession in the first sentence is tone; the second sentence is direction.

Compare the two prompts on what matters for research:

| | Weak prompt | Strong prompt |
|---|---|---|
| Construct defined? | No — the model guesses | Yes — your codebook, verbatim |
| Output parseable? | No — prose | Yes — fixed JSON schema |
| Edge cases handled? | No | Decision rule for hedged speeches |
| Auditable? | No | Evidence quote you can check against the text |
| Same coding tomorrow? | Unlikely | Much more likely (with `temperature=0` where the model accepts it) |

Notice that nothing in the strong prompt is clever. There is no persona flourish, no tip, no magic phrase. It is a codebook with a format attached. That is the point.

## The codebook is the prompt

That worked example scales into the general recipe for social science annotation: **take the codebook you would hand a new research assistant and make it the system message.** If you have run a human coding project, you already know what belongs in it — and if your codebook cannot survive being pasted into a prompt, it was probably too vague for your RAs too.

A codebook-as-prompt needs, at minimum:

1. **The unit of analysis.** One speech? One paragraph? Models will happily code the wrong unit if you let them.
2. **Category definitions** — one or two sentences each, with the conceptual boundary stated, not implied.
3. **Decision rules for the known hard cases.** Hedged positions, sarcasm, quoted opponents ("the honourable member says we should open the borders — nonsense!"), procedural remarks. Every rule you write here is a disagreement you do not have to adjudicate later.
4. **A fallback rule.** What to do when uncertain. "If no policy direction is discernible, code neutral" is a rule; silence is a coin flip.
5. **Two or three worked examples**, class-balanced, including at least one hard case with the correct label.

::: {.callout-tip}
Draft the prompt as if the reader were a smart new RA on day one: no shared context, no charitable interpretation, no opportunity to ask questions. Then pilot it on 20–50 documents you have already hand-coded and read every disagreement. Most disagreements at this stage are not model failures — they are holes in your codebook that human coders were papering over with common sense. Fixing them improves the prompt *and* the codebook.
:::

## In code

Assemble the pieces. The plumbing is all from @sec-apis; what is new is only how the prompt is built.

```{python}
import json
from openai import OpenAI
from google.colab import userdata

CODEBOOK = """You are a political scientist coding parliamentary speeches.

Task: code the speech's stance toward immigration levels.

Categories:
- restrictive: favors reducing immigration or tightening controls,
  including hedged framings whose policy direction is restrictive.
- expansive: favors maintaining or increasing immigration, or
  expanding rights of immigrants.
- neutral: purely procedural, descriptive, or genuinely balanced.

Decision rules:
- Code the policy direction, not the tone.
- A speech quoting an opponent's position takes the speaker's own stance.
- If no policy direction is discernible, code neutral.

Return JSON with keys "stance" and "evidence" (a short verbatim quote)."""

# Few-shot examples: class-balanced, one hard case per class where possible.
FEWSHOT = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "Speech: 'The backlog in asylum processing "
        "shames us all; we must fund the tribunals properly.'"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": '{"stance": "expansive", '
        '"evidence": "we must fund the tribunals properly"}'},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Speech: 'I rise to move that the debate "
        "be adjourned until the committee reports.'"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": '{"stance": "neutral", '
        '"evidence": "the debate be adjourned"}'},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Speech: 'Nobody wants to slam the door, "
        "but compassion without control is not a policy.'"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": '{"stance": "restrictive", '
        '"evidence": "compassion without control is not a policy"}'},
]

schema = {
    "name": "stance_annotation",
    "schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "stance": {"type": "string",
                       "enum": ["restrictive", "expansive", "neutral"]},
            "evidence": {"type": "string"},
        },
        "required": ["stance", "evidence"],
        "additionalProperties": False,
    },
    "strict": True,
}

client = OpenAI(api_key=userdata.get("OPENAI_API_KEY"))

def code_speech(text, model="gpt-5-mini"):
    r = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,               # gpt-5 series: temperature not accepted
        messages=[{"role": "system", "content": CODEBOOK},
                  *FEWSHOT,
                  {"role": "user", "content": f"Speech: '{text}'"}],
        response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
    )
    return json.loads(r.choices[0].message.content)
```

The same prompt travels unchanged to other providers — Anthropic wants the codebook as its `system` argument, and open-weight models are one `base_url` away:

```{python}
import anthropic

anthropic_client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=userdata.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"))

def code_speech_claude(text, model="claude-haiku-4-5"):
    r = anthropic_client.messages.create(
        model=model, max_tokens=100, temperature=0,
        system=CODEBOOK,
        messages=[*FEWSHOT, {"role": "user", "content": f"Speech: '{text}'"}],
    )
    return json.loads(r.content[0].text)

openrouter = OpenAI(base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1",
                    api_key=userdata.get("OPENROUTER_API_KEY"))

def code_speech_open(text, model="meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct"):
    r = openrouter.chat.completions.create(
        model=model, temperature=0,
        messages=[{"role": "system", "content": CODEBOOK},
                  *FEWSHOT,
                  {"role": "user", "content": f"Speech: '{text}'"}],
    )
    return json.loads(r.choices[0].message.content)
```

<!-- DRAFT NOTE (Emre): model names churn — verify gpt-5-mini / claude-haiku-4-5 / the llama slug are current at publication, same note as the APIs chapter. -->

One design habit to notice: `CODEBOOK` and `FEWSHOT` are named constants, defined once, separate from the plumbing. That is not just tidiness. It means the prompt has a single authoritative version you can diff, version-control, and — the point of the next two sections — hold fixed while you test everything else. Scaling this loop to 10,000 speeches, with checkpointing and retries, is the subject of @sec-batch.

## Prompt sensitivity is a validity threat

Here is the section that earns the chapter its skeptical title. Everything above treats the prompt as a fixed instrument. It is not — and the instability is worse than most users assume.

The evidence is consistent and unflattering. [Sclar et al. (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.11324) found that *formatting alone* — separators, casing, spacing, the parts of a prompt nobody thinks of as content — produced accuracy swings of up to 76 points in few-shot classification with open models, and the sensitivity did not go away with bigger models or instruction tuning. [Zhao et al. (2021)](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/zhao21c/zhao21c.pdf) showed the same for example order and class balance. [Salinas and Morstatter (2024)](https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-acl.275/) found label changes from perturbations as small as adding a space at the end of the prompt. And [Reiss (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.11085) showed that even *holding the prompt constant*, repeated runs of the same classification could fall below conventional reliability thresholds — a caution to run alongside the annotation success stories.

For a social scientist this has a precise name: a threat to measurement validity. If your estimate of restrictive-speech share moves three points when you paraphrase the codebook, then "restrictive-speech share" as you measured it is partly an artifact of wording — Rugg's forbid/allow gap, running at machine speed over your whole corpus. You cannot eliminate the sensitivity. You can measure it. The practice, adapted from [Törnberg's (2024) checklist](https://sociologica.unibo.it/article/view/19461):

Write three to five variants of your final prompt *that any competent colleague would accept as the same instrument* — paraphrased instructions, reordered categories, reordered few-shot examples. Run all of them on your human-coded validation sample. Then report the spread, not just the best number. A hypothetical but realistic robustness table for our running example, on a 200-speech validation sample:

| Prompt variant | Agreement with human labels | % coded restrictive |
|---|---:|---:|
| V1 — final codebook prompt | .84 | 41% |
| V2 — instructions paraphrased | .83 | 42% |
| V3 — categories in reverse order | .82 | 40% |
| V4 — categories renamed A / B / C | .71 | 33% |

(These numbers are illustrative, not from a real run — but the pattern is the one you should expect.) V1–V3 telling the same story is the robustness you report. V4 is the diagnostic bonus: renaming the categories to content-free labels costs 13 points, which tells you the model is leaning on the *meanings of the label words themselves* — "restrictive" as a word is doing work your definitions should be doing. That is not a reason to use V4; it is a reason to sharpen the definitions.

::: {.callout-warning}
If your *substantive conclusion* — the direction of a trend, the sign of a difference between parties — flips across reasonable paraphrases, you do not have a finding with a caveat. You do not have a measurement. Fix the instrument before you interpret anything downstream of it.
:::

## Pre-register the prompt

The final step turns all of this from advice into discipline. A prompt you keep editing while looking at results is a garden of forking paths: with enough tweaks, some wording will produce the pattern you hoped for, and you will have no way to know whether you measured the world or engineered the answer. Survey researchers solved this problem by convention — you do not rewrite the questionnaire mid-fieldwork — and the same convention transfers directly:

1. **Draft** the codebook prompt from your human codebook.
2. **Pilot freely** on a development sample (50–100 hand-coded documents). Iterate as much as you like; this is instrument design, and it is supposed to be messy.
3. **Freeze**: the exact prompt text, few-shot examples, schema, model ID, and temperature, in version control. This is your instrument. Ideally, register it — in your preregistration if the study has one, or minimally as a time-stamped commit.
4. **Validate once** on a fresh hand-coded sample that played no role in development, running the frozen prompt plus its robustness variants. Report agreement statistics as you would for a human coder, and the variant spread.
5. **Only then** run the full corpus, with the frozen prompt, and report its full text verbatim in the appendix or repository — along with the variants you tried and discarded.

Step 4's "fresh sample" is the part people skip and should not. A prompt tuned on the same documents used to validate it is overfit in exactly the way a model tuned on its test set is; the agreement number becomes advertising, not evidence. And if validation reveals the frozen prompt is not good enough, the honest move is the expensive one: revise, re-freeze, and validate on *another* fresh sample. When no amount of prompt revision closes the gap, that is your signal to consider fine-tuning (@sec-fine-tuning) instead — and when the failure is missing knowledge rather than a fuzzy construct, the fix is context, not wording: that is retrieval-augmented generation (@sec-rag), next chapter.

<!-- DRAFT NOTE (Emre): opinion paragraph here — where you stand on preregistration norms for LLM measurement in political science, and whether journals are starting to require prompt appendices. -->

## In research

The payoff for all this care is that prompted LLMs have become a usable measurement instrument across the field. [Gilardi, Alizadeh, and Kubli (2023)](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2305016120) found ChatGPT beating MTurk crowd workers on annotating tweets and news articles for stance, topic, and framing — with zero-shot prompts built, in effect, from their human coder instructions. [Törnberg (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.06588) found GPT-4 matching or exceeding experts and crowd workers in inferring the party of US politicians from single tweets, including cases requiring contextual inference. Against that, keep [Ziems et al. (2024)](https://aclanthology.org/2024.cl-1.8/) in view: across 25 tasks, zero-shot models reach fair-to-moderate agreement with humans but fail to beat classifiers fine-tuned on labeled data. Prompting wins where labeled data is scarce, constructs are still moving, or iteration speed matters — which is, honestly, most of the exploratory phase of most projects.

What the prompting-specific craft in this chapter adds is breadth and defensibility. A codebook-as-prompt can be rewritten in an afternoon, which means you can code the same manifesto corpus for populism today and for welfare chauvinism tomorrow without retraining anything — across decades and, with capable models, across languages. Open-ended survey responses that used to be skimmed and abandoned become codable at scale; interview transcripts get a first pass against a qualitative codebook with the researcher auditing evidence quotes rather than producing every label; media corpora get frame-coded across years of coverage with the same frozen instrument, so over-time comparisons are not confounded by coder drift.

The same flexibility is the hazard. Because prompts are cheap to change, they invite exactly the undisciplined iteration that the pre-registration workflow above forbids, and because outputs are fluent, they invite trust that the sensitivity literature says is unearned. The emerging reporting standard — validate against humans on a meaningful fresh sample, report prompt variants, publish the exact prompt, pin the model version — is this chapter in one sentence. Treat the prompt as you were trained to treat a survey instrument, and most of what can go wrong here becomes a known problem with a known fix.

<!-- DRAFT NOTE (Emre): add your own project example here — the corpus, the prompt variant that moved the estimate, and what you reported. The section needs one real war story. -->

## Further reading

- [Brown et al. (2020), "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners," *NeurIPS*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165) — where in-context learning enters; the origin of few-shot prompting.
- [Wei et al. (2022)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903) and [Kojima et al. (2022)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.11916) — chain-of-thought, few-shot and zero-shot; then [Sprague et al. (2025), *ICLR*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.12183) for where it actually helps.
- [Schulhoff et al. (2024), "The Prompt Report"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.06608) — the systematic survey; useful as a map and as a caution about how much is untested.
- [Sclar et al. (2024), *ICLR*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.11324), [Zhao et al. (2021), *ICML*](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/zhao21c/zhao21c.pdf), and [Salinas & Morstatter (2024), *Findings of ACL*](https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-acl.275/) — the prompt-sensitivity evidence base.
- [Gilardi, Alizadeh & Kubli (2023), *PNAS*](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2305016120), [Törnberg (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.06588), and [Reiss (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.11085) — the annotation results, positive and cautionary, for social science texts.
- [Ziems et al. (2024), "Can Large Language Models Transform Computational Social Science?" *Computational Linguistics*](https://aclanthology.org/2024.cl-1.8/) — the broad benchmark; calibrates expectations.
- [Törnberg (2024), "Best Practices for Text Annotation with Large Language Models," *Sociologica*](https://sociologica.unibo.it/article/view/19461) — the checklist this chapter's validation and reporting advice follows; see also the [how-to guide](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.13106).
- [The Prompt Engineering Guide](https://www.promptingguide.ai/) — a practitioner reference for techniques beyond this chapter's scope; read it with this chapter's skepticism in hand.
